key_distinguishers: All lesions at same stage of development (synchronous)
recommended_tests: Immediate WHO/national public health emergency pathway — BSL-4 only
DO NOT attempt routine lab — activate bioterrorism protocol
immediate_actions: Contact tracing of all contacts since rash onset
case_definition.who_basis: IHR 2005 — always notifiable. WHO smallpox eradication surveillance guidance.

payload.name: Smallpox
key_distinguishers: All lesions at same stage of development (synchronous)
recommended_tests: Immediate WHO/national public health emergency pathway — BSL-4 only
DO NOT attempt routine lab — activate bioterrorism protocol
immediate_actions: Contact tracing of all contacts since rash onset
payload.who_basis: IHR 2005 — always notifiable. WHO smallpox eradication surveillance guidance.

who_case_definition.suspected: ANY PERSON with febrile prodrome (2-4 days before rash) followed by centrifugal vesicular/pustular rash appearing simultaneously on face, arms, and trunk — with all lesions at the same stage of development.
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case with no alternative diagnosis and negative monkeypox result.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Variola virus detected by PCR or electron microscopy in specialized BSL-4 laboratory.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: IHR Tier 1 — ALWAYS NOTIFIABLE. BIOTERRORISM PROTOCOL.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Smallpox Preparedness; IHR Annex 2.
who_case_definition.poe_action: IMMEDIATE FULL ISOLATION. DO NOT RELEASE TRAVELLER. Notify WHO NFP and national emergency contacts IMMEDIATELY.

recommended_tests: Coronavirus PCR via public health reference laboratory
case_definition.who_basis: IHR 2005 — always notifiable. Retained in scoring engine per IHR legal obligation.

Mandell, Douglas, and Bennett's Principles of Infectious Diseases, 9th Edition
sources.notes: LR-calibrated symptom weights

sources.notes: Prevalence calibration in symptomatic returning travellers

payload.name: Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome (SARS)
recommended_tests: Coronavirus PCR via public health reference laboratory
payload.who_basis: IHR 2005 — always notifiable. Retained in scoring engine per IHR legal obligation.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with unexplained severe acute respiratory illness (fever >38°C + cough/difficulty breathing) AND epidemiological link (travel to affected area or contact with SARS case within 10 days).
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case with CXR/CT showing pneumonia or ARDS, AND no alternative diagnosis.
who_case_definition.confirmed: SARS-CoV RNA by PCR (at least 2 different clinical specimens, or same specimen on ≥2 occasions).
who_case_definition.ihr_category: IHR Tier 1 — ALWAYS NOTIFIABLE.
who_case_definition.source: WHO SARS Case Definition 2003; IHR Annex 2.

recommended_tests: Influenza PCR + subtyping via national reference lab
immediate_actions: Contact tracing of all poultry/bird exposures
case_definition.who_basis: IHR 2005 — always notifiable. WHO zoonotic influenza risk assessment guidance.

payload.name: Zoonotic / Novel Influenza (H5N1, H7N9 etc.)
recommended_tests: Influenza PCR + subtyping via national reference lab
immediate_actions: Contact tracing of all poultry/bird exposures
payload.who_basis: IHR 2005 — always notifiable. WHO zoonotic influenza risk assessment guidance.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with fever ≥38°C AND cough/difficulty breathing AND exposure to live or dead poultry/pigs/animals in an affected area, OR contact with confirmed H5N1/H7N9/novel influenza case, within 10 days.
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case with influenza A positive rapid test but negative subtyping for seasonal H1/H3.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Novel influenza virus identified by real-time RT-PCR using WHO-recommended assay.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: IHR Tier 1 — ALWAYS NOTIFIABLE regardless of number of cases.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Influenza at the Human-Animal Interface 2024; IHR Annex 2.

recommended_tests: Two stool specimens 24-48h apart for AFP investigation
case_definition.who_basis: IHR 2005 — always notifiable. WHO GPEI AFP surveillance case definition.

payload.name: Poliomyelitis / Acute Flaccid Paralysis
recommended_tests: Two stool specimens 24-48h apart for AFP investigation
payload.who_basis: IHR 2005 — always notifiable. WHO GPEI AFP surveillance case definition.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any child <15 years with acute flaccid paralysis (AFP), OR any person with AFP suspected to be polio regardless of age.
who_case_definition.probable: AFP case with stool specimen not adequate, OR AFP case with epidemiological link to confirmed case.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Wild poliovirus OR circulating vaccine-derived poliovirus (cVDPV) isolated from stool specimen from AFP case.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: IHR Tier 1 — ALWAYS NOTIFIABLE. Single confirmed wild poliovirus case = PHEIC.
who_case_definition.source: WHO GPEI AFP Surveillance Guidelines; IHR Annex 2.

case_definition.who_basis: WHO Global Cholera and AGE Control Coalition. IHR Annex 2 epidemic-prone disease.

payload.name: Cholera
payload.who_basis: WHO Global Cholera and AGE Control Coalition. IHR Annex 2 epidemic-prone disease.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person aged ≥5 years with acute profuse watery (rice-water) diarrhoea with or without vomiting, AND signs of severe dehydration OR shock, in an area with known cholera activity — OR any person with profuse rice-water stool regardless of age in an epidemic setting.
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case with epidemiological link to a confirmed case, AND positive laboratory results pending.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Any suspected case in which Vibrio cholerae O1 or O139 is isolated from stool by culture, OR a positive cholera RDT in a non-endemic context.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: IHR Annex 2 — Notifiable to WHO if unexpected or unusual.
who_case_definition.source: WHO AFRO IDSR 2021 Technical Guidelines, Chapter 2.

key_distinguishers: Jaundice + bleeding + renewed fever = toxic phase
recommended_tests: Yellow fever PCR (early) / IgM serology (after day 5)
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Yellow Fever fact sheet. IHR Annex 2 epidemic-prone disease. Required vaccination at POE.

payload.name: Yellow fever
key_distinguishers: Jaundice + bleeding + renewed fever = toxic phase
recommended_tests: Yellow fever PCR (early) / IgM serology (after day 5)
payload.who_basis: WHO Yellow Fever fact sheet. IHR Annex 2 epidemic-prone disease. Required vaccination at POE.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person presenting with acute onset of fever FOLLOWED by jaundice within 14 days of first symptoms, in an area with yellow fever risk OR with recent travel to an endemic area.
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case with positive serology (IgM by ELISA) AND no recent YF vaccination, OR epidemiological link to a confirmed case.
who_case_definition.confirmed: A probable case with: virus isolation from blood/tissue, OR YF antigen or RNA detected by PCR, OR seroconversion in paired samples (4-fold rise in neutralizing antibodies).
who_case_definition.ihr_category: IHR Annex 2 — Always notifiable to WHO.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Yellow Fever Fact Sheet 2023; IHR Annex 2.

recommended_tests: Ebola PCR per national/WHO algorithm (whole blood in outbreak context)
DO NOT obtain blood without full PPE
immediate_actions: FULL PPE (coverall + N95 + face shield + double gloves)
Contact trace all contacts within 21 days
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Ebola disease fact sheet. VHF red-flag logic per IHR Annex 2.

payload.name: Ebola Virus Disease (EVD)
recommended_tests: Ebola PCR per national/WHO algorithm (whole blood in outbreak context)
DO NOT obtain blood without full PPE
immediate_actions: FULL PPE (coverall + N95 + face shield + double gloves)
Contact trace all contacts within 21 days
payload.who_basis: WHO Ebola disease fact sheet. VHF red-flag logic per IHR Annex 2.

who_case_definition.suspected: ANY PERSON with acute onset of fever ≥38°C AND contact with blood/body fluids/secretions of a person with EVD, OR deceased person who had EVD, OR contact with bats/primates in endemic areas — OR unexplained hemorrhagic fever.
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case evaluated by a clinician, OR a deceased case with epidemiological link to a confirmed case, where laboratory testing cannot be performed.
who_case_definition.confirmed: A suspected or probable case with: Ebola virus RNA detected by RT-PCR (≥2 independent target amplification), OR Ebola virus antigen detected by ELISA, OR Ebola virus-specific IgM or IgG serology, OR virus isolated by cell culture.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: IHR Annex 2 — ALWAYS notifiable to WHO. IMMEDIATE notification required.
who_case_definition.source: WHO EVD Case Definition 2023; IHR Annex 2.
who_case_definition.poe_action: IMMEDIATE ISOLATION. Full PPE (PAPR or N95 + face shield + gown + double gloves + boot covers). Notify national IHR focal point IMMEDIATELY.

case_definition.who_basis: WHO Marburg virus disease fact sheet.

payload.name: Marburg Virus Disease (MVD)
payload.who_basis: WHO Marburg virus disease fact sheet.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with fever ≥38.5°C AND ≥1 of: headache, severe malaise, myalgia — AND contact with someone with Marburg disease, OR exposure to bats/mines/caves in endemic area — OR any unexplained hemorrhagic fever with no alternative diagnosis.
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case not confirmed by laboratory testing, AND epidemiological link to a confirmed case or exposure to high-risk bat habitat.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Marburg virus RNA detected by RT-PCR, OR Marburg antigen detected by immunohistochemistry, OR virus isolated by culture.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: IHR Annex 2 — ALWAYS notifiable. Highest biosafety priority.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Marburg Case Definition 2023; IHR Annex 2.

key_distinguishers: Mastomys rat exposure — West Africa endemic
recommended_tests: Lassa PCR / serology per national algorithm
immediate_actions: Urgent ribavirin referral if high clinical suspicion
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Lassa fever fact sheet. Endemic in West Africa Mastomys rat belt.

payload.name: Lassa fever
key_distinguishers: Mastomys rat exposure — West Africa endemic
recommended_tests: Lassa PCR / serology per national algorithm
immediate_actions: Urgent ribavirin referral if high clinical suspicion
payload.who_basis: WHO Lassa fever fact sheet. Endemic in West Africa Mastomys rat belt.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with fever ≥38°C for ≥2 days AND ≥1 of: sore throat with exudate, myalgia, hemorrhagic manifestations, encephalopathy, proteinuria — in an endemic area OR with travel from an endemic area within 21 days.
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case with positive Lassa antigen RDT, OR epidemiological link to confirmed case.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Lassa virus RNA detected by RT-PCR, OR Lassa IgM ≥1:8 by ELISA, OR seroconversion in paired sera, OR virus isolation.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: IHR Annex 2 equivalent — Notifiable to WHO NFP.
who_case_definition.source: WHO AFRO IDSR 2021; WHO Lassa Fact Sheet 2017.

key_distinguishers: Tick bite or livestock slaughter exposure
recommended_tests: CCHF PCR or serology per BSL-3/national reference lab
case_definition.who_basis: WHO CCHF fact sheet 2024. IHR Annex 2 — requires special attention.

payload.name: Crimean-Congo Haemorrhagic Fever (CCHF)
key_distinguishers: Tick bite or livestock slaughter exposure
recommended_tests: CCHF PCR or serology per BSL-3/national reference lab
payload.who_basis: WHO CCHF fact sheet 2024. IHR Annex 2 — requires special attention.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with acute onset of fever AND hemorrhagic manifestations AND history of tick bite OR exposure to livestock/slaughterhouses in a CCHF-endemic country within 14 days.
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case with positive CCHF serology (IgM) AND no alternative diagnosis.
who_case_definition.confirmed: CCHF virus RNA by RT-PCR, OR virus antigen by ELISA, OR seroconversion/4-fold rise in IgG.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: IHR Annex 2 equivalent.
who_case_definition.source: WHO CCHF Fact Sheet 2022; ECDC Technical Guidance.

key_distinguishers: Most cases mild — minority develop haemorrhage or encephalitis
immediate_actions: Urgent referral if haemorrhagic or neurological signs present

payload.name: Rift Valley fever (RVF)
key_distinguishers: Most cases mild — minority develop haemorrhage or encephalitis
immediate_actions: Urgent referral if haemorrhagic or neurological signs present
payload.who_basis: WHO Rift Valley fever fact sheet. IHR Annex 2 epidemic-prone.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with acute febrile illness in an endemic area AND exposure to animal blood/tissues/livestock, OR involvement in veterinary activities, during an RVF outbreak.
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case with positive serology (IgM ≥1:100 by ELISA).
who_case_definition.confirmed: RVF virus RNA by RT-PCR, OR virus isolation, OR seroconversion in paired samples.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: IHR Annex 2 equivalent — especially if animal epizootic confirmed.
who_case_definition.source: WHO RVF Fact Sheet 2020.

key_distinguishers: LYMPHADENOPATHY — the major differentiator from smallpox
recommended_tests: Mpox PCR from lesion swab (ideally roof of lesion or lesion fluid)
immediate_actions: Isolate — airborne precautions for clade Ib
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Mpox fact sheet 2024. WHO PHEIC declared 2022 (lifted 2023). New PHEIC August 2024 (clade Ib).

payload.name: Mpox (Monkeypox)
key_distinguishers: LYMPHADENOPATHY — the major differentiator from smallpox
recommended_tests: Mpox PCR from lesion swab (ideally roof of lesion or lesion fluid)
immediate_actions: Isolate — airborne precautions for clade Ib
payload.who_basis: WHO Mpox fact sheet 2024. WHO PHEIC declared 2022 (lifted 2023). New PHEIC August 2024 (clade Ib).

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with unexplained acute skin rash, lesions or ulcers AND at least ONE of: headache, fever ≥38°C, lymphadenopathy, myalgia, arthralgia, asthenia — AND no alternative diagnosis to explain all features.
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case with epidemiological link to a confirmed or probable case, OR positive orthopoxvirus serology.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Mpox virus DNA detected by PCR (orthopoxvirus AND mpox-specific sequences), from skin lesion material (swab or crust) using validated assay.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: IHR Annex 2 (2022 outbreak PHEIC now ended — continue surveillance).
who_case_definition.source: WHO Mpox Case Definition 2024.

recommended_tests: Blood cultures (before antibiotics if safe to delay briefly)
immediate_actions: Immediate public health notification if cluster/outbreak

payload.name: Meningococcal meningitis
recommended_tests: Blood cultures (before antibiotics if safe to delay briefly)
immediate_actions: Immediate public health notification if cluster/outbreak
payload.who_basis: WHO AFRO IDSR meningitis case definition. WHO Meningitis Belt surveillance.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with sudden onset of fever AND stiff neck OR petechial/purpuric rash, OR any case of acute meningitis or meningoencephalitis.
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case with: CSF with cloudy appearance/elevated WBC/elevated protein/decreased glucose, OR gram-negative diplococci in CSF.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Neisseria meningitidis isolated from CSF/blood, OR meningococcal antigen detected, OR PCR positive.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: WHO AFRO priority disease — notifiable during Meningitis Belt dry season.
who_case_definition.source: WHO AFRO IDSR 2021; WHO meningitis guidelines.

recommended_tests: Measles IgM / PCR per national algorithm
Urine PCR if IgM negative in early rash

payload.name: Measles
recommended_tests: Measles IgM / PCR per national algorithm
Urine PCR if IgM negative in early rash
payload.who_basis: WHO AFRO IDSR measles case definition. WHO surveillance standard.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with fever AND generalized maculopapular rash lasting ≥3 days AND at least ONE of: cough, runny nose (coryza), red eyes (conjunctivitis).
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case with epidemiological link to a confirmed case AND no laboratory test performed.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Measles virus isolation, OR measles IgM positive (≥3 days after rash onset), OR ≥4-fold rise in measles IgG, OR measles RNA detected by PCR.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: WHO priority vaccine-preventable disease. PHEIC if vaccination coverage <80%.
who_case_definition.source: WHO AFRO IDSR 2021; WHO measles vaccine position paper 2023.

key_distinguishers: Congenital rubella syndrome risk in pregnancy
recommended_tests: Rubella IgM / PCR per national algorithm (especially in pregnancy)
immediate_actions: PREGNANCY RISK ASSESSMENT — congenital rubella syndrome
case_definition.who_basis: WHO rubella surveillance. Critical for CRS prevention.

payload.name: Rubella
key_distinguishers: Congenital rubella syndrome risk in pregnancy
recommended_tests: Rubella IgM / PCR per national algorithm (especially in pregnancy)
immediate_actions: PREGNANCY RISK ASSESSMENT — congenital rubella syndrome
payload.who_basis: WHO rubella surveillance. Critical for CRS prevention.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with acute generalized maculopapular rash AND arthralgia/arthritis OR suboccipital/postauricular/occipital lymphadenopathy — AND unvaccinated OR unknown vaccination status.
who_case_definition.probable: Suspected case with epidemiological link to confirmed rubella.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Rubella IgM positive (≥3 days after rash onset), OR ≥4-fold IgG rise, OR rubella RNA detected.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: Critical: Congenital Rubella Syndrome in unimmunized pregnant women — teratogenic.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Rubella Position Paper 2020.

key_distinguishers: Haemoptysis = pneumonic plague until excluded in endemic area
Flea/rodent exposure OR secondary from bubonic
recommended_tests: Plague testing via national/WHO reference laboratory (BSL-2+)
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Plague fact sheet. IHR Annex 2 epidemic-prone disease.

payload.name: Pneumonic plague
key_distinguishers: Haemoptysis = pneumonic plague until excluded in endemic area
Flea/rodent exposure OR secondary from bubonic
recommended_tests: Plague testing via national/WHO reference laboratory (BSL-2+)
payload.who_basis: WHO Plague fact sheet. IHR Annex 2 epidemic-prone disease.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with acute onset of fever, chills, headache, cough with blood-tinged sputum — especially if contact with plague cases or exposure to rodents/fleas in endemic areas within 7 days.
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case with laboratory results consistent with Y. pestis (F1 antigen positive by RDT).
who_case_definition.confirmed: Yersinia pestis identified by culture, PCR, or ≥4-fold rise in F1 antigen antibody titer.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: IHR Annex 2 — Notifiable to WHO. Pneumonic plague = IMMEDIATE isolation.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Plague Fact Sheet 2022; IHR Annex 2.

key_distinguishers: Can progress to septicaemic or pneumonic plague
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Plague fact sheet. Madagascar, DRC, Tanzania are endemic.

payload.name: Bubonic plague
key_distinguishers: Can progress to septicaemic or pneumonic plague
payload.who_basis: WHO Plague fact sheet. Madagascar, DRC, Tanzania are endemic.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with fever, painful swollen lymph nodes (buboes) — especially if in plague-endemic area or flea/rodent exposure within 7 days.
who_case_definition.probable: F1 antigen rapid test positive.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Y. pestis isolated from blood/bubo aspirate/sputum, OR 4-fold rise in F1 antibody.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: IHR Annex 2 equivalent. Potential for pneumonic conversion.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Plague Fact Sheet 2022.

key_distinguishers: Fruit bat contact / date palm sap in South/SE Asia
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Nipah virus Blueprint pathogen. Highest pandemic potential per WHO R&D Blueprint.

payload.name: Nipah Virus Disease
key_distinguishers: Fruit bat contact / date palm sap in South/SE Asia
payload.who_basis: WHO Nipah virus Blueprint pathogen. Highest pandemic potential per WHO R&D Blueprint.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with encephalitis (fever + altered consciousness/seizures) AND contact with pigs, bats, or bat-contaminated food (date palm sap) in endemic area (Bangladesh, India) within 21 days.
who_case_definition.probable: Suspected case with epidemiological link to confirmed exposure.
who_case_definition.confirmed: NiV RNA by RT-PCR, OR NiV-specific IgM, OR virus isolation.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: CFR 40-75%. WHO R&D Blueprint priority pathogen. Person-to-person transmission in Bangladesh strains.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Nipah Fact Sheet 2022.

key_distinguishers: Rodent exposure (Hantaan, Seoul, Sin Nombre virus)
recommended_tests: Hantavirus serology / PCR per reference laboratory
immediate_actions: Supportive care + ICU referral if respiratory failure
No person-to-person isolation needed for most strains
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Hantavirus fact sheet. Emerging zoonotic — global distribution.

payload.name: Hantavirus (HPS / HFRS)
key_distinguishers: Rodent exposure (Hantaan, Seoul, Sin Nombre virus)
recommended_tests: Hantavirus serology / PCR per reference laboratory
immediate_actions: Supportive care + ICU referral if respiratory failure
No person-to-person isolation needed for most strains
payload.who_basis: WHO Hantavirus fact sheet. Emerging zoonotic — global distribution.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with fever, severe headache, AND ONE of: hemorrhagic manifestations, renal failure, thrombocytopenia (Old World: HFRS) — OR respiratory distress with bilateral pulmonary infiltrates after rodent exposure (New World: HCPS).
who_case_definition.probable: Hantavirus IgM positive by ELISA.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Hantavirus RNA by PCR, OR ≥4-fold IgG rise, OR immunohistochemistry on tissue.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: Not IHR notifiable. CFR varies: 1% (HFRS) to 35% (HCPS). No specific antiviral.
who_case_definition.source: WHO/PAHO Hantavirus Fact Sheet 2022.

key_distinguishers: Camel or Arabian Peninsula healthcare exposure
recommended_tests: MERS-CoV PCR per national/WHO protocol (lower + upper respiratory)
case_definition.who_basis: WHO MERS-CoV fact sheet.

Mandell, Douglas, and Bennett's Principles of Infectious Diseases, 9th Edition
sources.notes: LR-calibrated symptom weights

payload.name: MERS-CoV (Middle East Respiratory Syndrome)
key_distinguishers: Camel or Arabian Peninsula healthcare exposure
recommended_tests: MERS-CoV PCR per national/WHO protocol (lower + upper respiratory)
payload.who_basis: WHO MERS-CoV fact sheet.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with fever AND acute respiratory illness (cough or shortness of breath) AND travel from/to or residence in the Arabian Peninsula within 14 days, OR close contact with a confirmed MERS case.
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case with influenza/other respiratory viruses ruled out, pending confirmation.
who_case_definition.confirmed: MERS-CoV RNA detected by rRT-PCR from respiratory specimen (lower respiratory preferred).
who_case_definition.ihr_category: IHR Annex 2 equivalent. Two-dose camel-related exposure cluster = immediate notification.
who_case_definition.source: WHO MERS Interim Case Definition 2023.

key_distinguishers: Dengue rash (3-5 days — islands of white in sea of red)
Warning signs for severe dengue: abdominal pain, vomiting, mucosal bleeding
immediate_actions: Monitor for warning signs (abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, plasma leakage)
Urgent referral if dengue shock suspected
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Dengue fact sheet 2024. WHO dengue clinical guidelines — severe dengue warning signs.

payload.name: Dengue
key_distinguishers: Dengue rash (3-5 days — islands of white in sea of red)
Warning signs for severe dengue: abdominal pain, vomiting, mucosal bleeding
immediate_actions: Monitor for warning signs (abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, plasma leakage)
Urgent referral if dengue shock suspected
payload.who_basis: WHO Dengue fact sheet 2024. WHO dengue clinical guidelines — severe dengue warning signs.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with acute fever (2–7 days) AND at least 2 of: nausea/vomiting, rash, myalgia/arthralgia, headache, positive tourniquet test, leukopenia, petechiae — in a dengue-endemic area OR with travel from one within 14 days.
who_case_definition.probable: Dengue rapid NS1 antigen positive, OR dengue IgM positive (≥5 days after fever onset).
who_case_definition.confirmed: Dengue virus RNA by RT-PCR, OR dengue NS1 antigen confirmed by ELISA, OR seroconversion IgM/IgG in paired sera.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: WHO AFRO priority disease. Not IHR Annex 2 but notifiable nationally.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Dengue Guidelines 2009 (revised 2012); PAHO 2022 Guidelines.

display_name: Severe Dengue (Dengue Haemorrhagic Fever / Shock Syndrome)

key_distinguishers: Plasma leakage signs: haemoconcentration, pleural effusion
Shock (cold clammy skin, narrowing pulse pressure)
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Dengue clinical management guidelines. Severe dengue warning signs criteria.

payload.name: Severe Dengue (Dengue Haemorrhagic Fever / Shock Syndrome)
key_distinguishers: Plasma leakage signs: haemoconcentration, pleural effusion
Shock (cold clammy skin, narrowing pulse pressure)
payload.who_basis: WHO Dengue clinical management guidelines. Severe dengue warning signs criteria.

who_case_definition.suspected: A dengue case with warning signs: abdominal pain/tenderness, persistent vomiting, clinical fluid accumulation, mucosal bleed, lethargy, liver enlargement >2cm, rising HCT with rapid platelet drop — OR severe dengue criteria (severe plasma leakage, bleeding, organ impairment).
who_case_definition.probable: Clinical severe dengue in confirmed dengue patient.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Confirmed dengue with severe dengue features.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: Requires immediate clinical management. CFR <1% with good care, up to 20% without.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Dengue: Guidelines for Diagnosis, Treatment, Prevention and Control 2012.

key_distinguishers: Cyclical fever (48h P. vivax/ovale, 72h P. malariae)
Travel to sub-Saharan Africa within 30 days
Most common cause of fever in returning travellers from Africa
immediate_actions: URGENT MALARIA TEST for any febrile traveller from endemic area
ACT (artemisinin-based combination therapy) per national protocol
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Malaria fact sheet 2024. Most common cause of fever in returning Africa travellers.

payload.name: Malaria (Uncomplicated)
key_distinguishers: Cyclical fever (48h P. vivax/ovale, 72h P. malariae)
Travel to sub-Saharan Africa within 30 days
Most common cause of fever in returning travellers from Africa
immediate_actions: URGENT MALARIA TEST for any febrile traveller from endemic area
ACT (artemisinin-based combination therapy) per national protocol
payload.who_basis: WHO Malaria fact sheet 2024. Most common cause of fever in returning Africa travellers.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with fever (≥37.5°C) OR history of fever within 48-72 hours AND NO signs of severe malaria — in an area with malaria risk or travel history.
who_case_definition.probable: Positive malaria RDT.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Plasmodium spp. identified by microscopy or PCR.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: Highly prevalent in endemic zones. Rapid RDT available for POE.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Guidelines for Treatment of Malaria 3rd Ed. 2015.

case_definition.who_basis: WHO Severe Malaria clinical management guidelines 2023.

payload.name: Severe Malaria (P. falciparum)
payload.who_basis: WHO Severe Malaria clinical management guidelines 2023.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any child OR adult with fever OR history of fever in last 48 hours AND living in or travel from malaria-endemic area — AND signs of severity: impaired consciousness, prostration, multiple convulsions, respiratory distress, abnormal bleeding, jaundice, hemoglobinuria, circulatory collapse, pulmonary edema.
who_case_definition.probable: Positive malaria RDT (PfHRP2 or pan-malaria).
who_case_definition.confirmed: Plasmodium falciparum identified by microscopy, PCR, or RDT with ≥1 sign of severity.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: Medical emergency. IV artesunate required. CFR 15-20% if untreated.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Guidelines for Treatment of Malaria 3rd Ed. 2015; WHO 2023 update.

key_distinguishers: Loss of taste/smell (anosmia) — strongest single differentiator
recommended_tests: PCR if negative RAT with high clinical suspicion
immediate_actions: Antivirals per national protocol if high risk
case_definition.who_basis: WHO COVID-19 illness surveillance. IHR notification obligations for unusual clusters.

payload.name: Typhoid fever (Enteric fever)
key_distinguishers: Step-ladder fever pattern — rises over 4-5 days
payload.who_basis: WHO AFRO IDSR typoid fever case definition. WHO typhoid fact sheet.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with fever ≥38.5°C for ≥3 days with no known focus AND living in or returning from a typhoid-endemic area.
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case with compatible clinical features AND positive Widal O-titer ≥1:160 in non-immune population (limited specificity).
who_case_definition.confirmed: S. Typhi or S. Paratyphi isolated from blood, bone marrow, stool, or urine culture.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: WHO priority disease. XDR typhoid (extensively drug-resistant) emerging in Pakistan.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Typhoid Fever Position Paper 2018.

key_distinguishers: Acute onset jaundice after faecal-oral exposure
Pre-icteric prodrome (fever, nausea, anorexia) 1-2 weeks before jaundice
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Hepatitis A fact sheet 2024.

payload.name: Hepatitis A
key_distinguishers: Acute onset jaundice after faecal-oral exposure
Pre-icteric prodrome (fever, nausea, anorexia) 1-2 weeks before jaundice
payload.who_basis: WHO Hepatitis A fact sheet 2024.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with acute onset of fever AND jaundice (or elevated ALT >10x upper limit of normal), AND exposure to contaminated food/water or HAV contact within 15-50 days.
who_case_definition.probable: Suspected case with epidemiological link to confirmed case.
who_case_definition.confirmed: HAV-specific IgM positive in acute serum.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: Self-limiting. Severe disease in elderly and immunocompromised. Not IHR notifiable.
who_case_definition.source: WHO HAV Position Paper 2012.

key_distinguishers: CFR 20-25% in PREGNANT WOMEN (critical POE concern)
recommended_tests: PREGNANCY TEST — if positive, classify as HIGH RISK IMMEDIATELY
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Hepatitis E fact sheet 2024. WHO EMRO hepatitis E in humanitarian settings.

payload.name: Hepatitis E
key_distinguishers: CFR 20-25% in PREGNANT WOMEN (critical POE concern)
recommended_tests: PREGNANCY TEST — if positive, classify as HIGH RISK IMMEDIATELY
payload.who_basis: WHO Hepatitis E fact sheet 2024. WHO EMRO hepatitis E in humanitarian settings.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with acute jaundice AND pruritus AND dark urine — AND no alternative diagnosis — in an area with poor WASH OR following flooding OR in a refugee/displaced population setting.
who_case_definition.probable: Suspected case with HEV IgM positive.
who_case_definition.confirmed: HEV RNA by RT-PCR in stool/serum, OR IgM with 4-fold IgG rise in paired sera.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: CFR up to 25% in pregnant women (3rd trimester) — PRIORITY for pregnant travellers.
who_case_definition.source: WHO HEV Position Paper 2015.

key_distinguishers: HYDROPHOBIA + AEROPHOBIA = PATHOGNOMONIC for rabies (virtually 100% specific)
POE role: identify, document, contact trace — not treatment
Long incubation makes this possible at any POE
recommended_tests: DFA/PCR from brain tissue post-mortem (clinical diagnosis pre-mortem)
Saliva/CSF/skin biopsy PCR during life — reference lab only
immediate_actions: EMERGENCY REFERRAL — palliative approach once symptomatic
ASSESS BITE HISTORY and PEP eligibility
Contact trace — identify animal and human contacts exposed
Notify public health for PEP distribution
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Rabies fact sheet 2024. Zero by 30 campaign.

payload.name: Rabies
key_distinguishers: HYDROPHOBIA + AEROPHOBIA = PATHOGNOMONIC for rabies (virtually 100% specific)
POE role: identify, document, contact trace — not treatment
Long incubation makes this possible at any POE
recommended_tests: DFA/PCR from brain tissue post-mortem (clinical diagnosis pre-mortem)
Saliva/CSF/skin biopsy PCR during life — reference lab only
immediate_actions: EMERGENCY REFERRAL — palliative approach once symptomatic
ASSESS BITE HISTORY and PEP eligibility
Contact trace — identify animal and human contacts exposed
Notify public health for PEP distribution
payload.who_basis: WHO Rabies fact sheet 2024. Zero by 30 campaign.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with acute encephalitis AND recent (within 1 year) bite/scratch from an animal in a rabies-endemic country — OR unexplained progressive encephalitis with hydrophobia, aerophobia, or aerophobia.
who_case_definition.probable: Direct fluorescence antibody (DFA) positive from skin biopsy (neck/nape) or corneal smear.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Rabies virus RNA by RT-PCR in saliva/CSF/brain tissue — or DFA on brain tissue post-mortem.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: Once symptomatic = virtually 100% fatal. PEP effective if started before symptoms.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Rabies Position Paper 2018.
who_case_definition.poe_action: If patient symptomatic with hydrophobia/aerophobia — immediate isolation + palliative care referral. Provide PEP history assessment.

key_distinguishers: Painless — the hallmark (differentiates from all other ulcers)
Black necrotic centre with oedema ring
Animal product exposure (hide, wool, bone meal)
Bioterrorism sentinel: multiple simultaneous cases = escalate immediately
recommended_tests: Swab from lesion for culture/PCR (Bacillus anthracis)
DO NOT INCISE — risk of systemic spread
immediate_actions: BIOTERRORISM ASSESSMENT if multiple simultaneous cases
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Anthrax fact sheet. Bioterrorism sentinel event.

payload.name: Cutaneous anthrax
key_distinguishers: Painless — the hallmark (differentiates from all other ulcers)
Black necrotic centre with oedema ring
Animal product exposure (hide, wool, bone meal)
Bioterrorism sentinel: multiple simultaneous cases = escalate immediately
recommended_tests: Swab from lesion for culture/PCR (Bacillus anthracis)
DO NOT INCISE — risk of systemic spread
immediate_actions: BIOTERRORISM ASSESSMENT if multiple simultaneous cases
payload.who_basis: WHO Anthrax fact sheet. Bioterrorism sentinel event.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with painless skin ulcer with central necrotic black eschar — especially following contact with animal hides, wool, meat, or soil in endemic areas.
who_case_definition.probable: Suspected case with epidemiological exposure and characteristic eschar.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Bacillus anthracis isolated from lesion/blood, OR PCR positive, OR immunohistochemistry on biopsy.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: Bioterrorism alert if cluster of cases without epidemiological explanation.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Anthrax Diagnostic Guidelines 2008.

key_distinguishers: Biphasic illness — brief improvement then rapid deterioration
Bioterrorism scenario: multiple simultaneous respiratory cases
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Anthrax fact sheet. Bioterrorism preparedness.

payload.name: Pulmonary / Inhalation anthrax
key_distinguishers: Biphasic illness — brief improvement then rapid deterioration
Bioterrorism scenario: multiple simultaneous respiratory cases
payload.who_basis: WHO Anthrax fact sheet. Bioterrorism preparedness.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with rapidly progressive respiratory distress + widened mediastinum on CXR + prodrome of mild fever/malaise — AND potential exposure to anthrax spores (occupational OR bioterrorism).
who_case_definition.probable: Suspected case with positive serology or antigen in early phase.
who_case_definition.confirmed: B. anthracis from blood/respiratory specimen, OR specific antibody confirmed.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: BIOTERRORISM PROTOCOL. CFR >85% without aggressive early treatment.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Anthrax 2008; CDC Emergency Preparedness Guidelines.
who_case_definition.poe_action: IMMEDIATE ISOLATION. Notify national health security authority and law enforcement.

key_distinguishers: Tick bite or wild animal contact
Pneumonic form if inhaled: severe respiratory
recommended_tests: PCR from skin lesion or lymph node
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Tularaemia fact sheet. CDC Tier A bioterrorism agent.

payload.name: Tularaemia (Rabbit fever)
key_distinguishers: Tick bite or wild animal contact
Pneumonic form if inhaled: severe respiratory
recommended_tests: PCR from skin lesion or lymph node
payload.who_basis: WHO Tularaemia fact sheet. CDC Tier A bioterrorism agent.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with sudden fever, headache, and ONE of: ulceroglandular (painful ulcer + swollen lymph node), oculoglandular (conjunctivitis + nodes), pneumonic (cough + chest pain) OR typhoidal form — with tick bite or animal (rabbit/hare) contact.
who_case_definition.probable: Positive Francisella serology ≥1:160.
who_case_definition.confirmed: F. tularensis isolated or PCR positive.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: Bioterrorism Category A agent (pneumonic form). Doxycycline treatment.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Guidelines on Tularemia 2007.

key_distinguishers: Eschar at tick bite site (spotted fevers + scrub typhus)
Rapid response to doxycycline = diagnostic + therapeutic
recommended_tests: PCR from eschar swab or blood (early)
immediate_actions: DOXYCYCLINE WITHOUT DELAY — do not wait for lab confirmation
case_definition.who_basis: WHO AFRO IDSR case definitions. CDC Rickettsia guidance.

payload.name: Rickettsiosis / Scrub typhus / Spotted fever
key_distinguishers: Eschar at tick bite site (spotted fevers + scrub typhus)
Rapid response to doxycycline = diagnostic + therapeutic
recommended_tests: PCR from eschar swab or blood (early)
immediate_actions: DOXYCYCLINE WITHOUT DELAY — do not wait for lab confirmation
payload.who_basis: WHO AFRO IDSR case definitions. CDC Rickettsia guidance.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with fever AND headache AND myalgia, AND presence of eschar (painless black necrotic ulcer) at site of chigger bite — in endemic area (Tsutsugamushi Triangle).
who_case_definition.probable: Suspected case with Scrub typhus rapid test positive OR IFA IgM ≥1:50.
who_case_definition.confirmed: IFA IgG ≥1:800 in convalescent serum, OR PCR positive for Orientia tsutsugamushi, OR isolation.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: Major cause of undifferentiated fever in Asia-Pacific. Doxycycline effective if started early.
who_case_definition.source: APCMV Guidelines 2023; Clin Infect Dis 2022.

case_definition.who_basis: WHO AFRO IDSR brucellosis case definition. Neglected zoonosis.

payload.name: Brucellosis
payload.who_basis: WHO AFRO IDSR brucellosis case definition. Neglected zoonosis.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with prolonged fever (>10 days), sweating (especially nocturnal), myalgia, arthralgia, and fatigue — AND occupational or dietary exposure (livestock, veterinary work, unpasteurized dairy, abattoir) in an endemic country.
who_case_definition.probable: Suspected case with Brucella agglutination test ≥1:160 in single serum.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Brucella spp. isolated from blood/bone marrow/tissue, OR ≥4-fold rise in agglutination titer.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: Significant occupational disease. Not IHR notifiable but important at agricultural POEs.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Brucellosis in Humans and Animals 2006.

key_distinguishers: Severe calf muscle tenderness (pathognomonic + highly specific)
Fresh water/flood contact or rodent exposure
Weil's disease: jaundice + renal failure + bleeding
East Africa: floods + agricultural workers high risk
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Leptospirosis burden epidemiology reference group.

payload.name: Leptospirosis
key_distinguishers: Severe calf muscle tenderness (pathognomonic + highly specific)
Fresh water/flood contact or rodent exposure
Weil's disease: jaundice + renal failure + bleeding
East Africa: floods + agricultural workers high risk
payload.who_basis: WHO Leptospirosis burden epidemiology reference group.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with acute febrile illness AND myalgia/muscle tenderness AND at least 1 of: calf muscle pain, conjunctival suffusion (red eyes without discharge), oliguria/anuria, jaundice — AND exposure to water/soil/animals OR flooding within 30 days.
who_case_definition.probable: Suspected case with positive Leptospira MAT ≥1:100 in single acute sample.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Leptospira isolated from blood/CSF/urine, OR ≥4-fold rise in MAT titer in paired sera.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: Weil disease (severe) requires ICU — CFR up to 40% with pulmonary hemorrhage syndrome.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Leptospirosis Human Health Fact Sheet 2011; ILS Guidelines.

key_distinguishers: Epidemic encephalitis in Asia + parts of Africa
< 1% of infections symptomatic — but severe in those affected
recommended_tests: JE IgM in CSF and serum
immediate_actions: Public health notification for cluster investigation
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Japanese Encephalitis fact sheet 2024.

sources.title: WHO AFRO IDSR Technical Guidelines 2021 (3rd Ed.)

payload.name: Japanese Encephalitis (JE)
key_distinguishers: Epidemic encephalitis in Asia + parts of Africa
< 1% of infections symptomatic — but severe in those affected
recommended_tests: JE IgM in CSF and serum
immediate_actions: Public health notification for cluster investigation
payload.who_basis: WHO Japanese Encephalitis fact sheet 2024.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with acute encephalitis syndrome (fever + altered mental status ± seizures) AND vaccination-unprotected status AND travel to/residence in JE-endemic region during transmission season.
who_case_definition.probable: Suspected case with positive JE IgM in CSF or serum.
who_case_definition.confirmed: JE virus RNA by RT-PCR in CSF, OR 4-fold rise in JE neutralizing antibody.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: CFR 20-30%. Neurological sequelae in 30-50% survivors. Vector: Culex tritaeniorhynchus.
who_case_definition.source: WHO JE Position Paper 2015.

recommended_tests: Antibiotic sensitivity — critical due to resistance
case_definition.who_basis: WHO AFRO IDSR dysentery case definition.

payload.name: Shigellosis (Bacillary dysentery)
recommended_tests: Antibiotic sensitivity — critical due to resistance
payload.who_basis: WHO AFRO IDSR dysentery case definition.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with acute diarrhoea with blood in stool (dysentery) AND fever AND tenesmus.
who_case_definition.probable: Suspected case with stool microscopy showing WBC ± RBC.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Shigella spp. isolated from stool culture.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: Important in refugee camps and post-disaster settings. Antimicrobial resistance emerging.
who_case_definition.source: WHO AFRO IDSR 2021. Treatment: Azithromycin or ciprofloxacin (check local resistance).

key_distinguishers: Acute onset watery diarrhoea without blood
Lower severity than cholera but high burden
recommended_tests: Cholera exclusion first in outbreak context
case_definition.who_basis: WHO AFRO IDSR acute diarrhoea case definition.

payload.name: Acute Watery Diarrhoea (Non-Cholera)
key_distinguishers: Acute onset watery diarrhoea without blood
Lower severity than cholera but high burden
recommended_tests: Cholera exclusion first in outbreak context
payload.who_basis: WHO AFRO IDSR acute diarrhoea case definition.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with ≥3 loose or liquid stools in 24 hours NOT meeting cholera criteria (no profuse rice-water stools, no cholera RDT positive).
who_case_definition.probable: N/A — usually treated clinically and confirmed by culture or PCR if available.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Stool culture identifies non-cholera pathogens (ETEC, EPEC, rotavirus, norovirus, etc.).
who_case_definition.ihr_category: Very common in POE settings post-flooding. Treat with ORS; escalate if dehydration.
who_case_definition.source: WHO AFRO IDSR 2021.

key_distinguishers: SUDDEN onset — patient remembers exact hour
Major differential for COVID-19 and zoonotic influenza
recommended_tests: Influenza rapid test or PCR if indicated
Subtype to rule out novel strain if severe
immediate_actions: Distinguish from zoonotic influenza if poultry exposure
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Global Influenza Programme. ILI definition.

sources.notes: LR-calibrated symptom weights

payload.name: Seasonal influenza
key_distinguishers: SUDDEN onset — patient remembers exact hour
Major differential for COVID-19 and zoonotic influenza
recommended_tests: Influenza rapid test or PCR if indicated
Subtype to rule out novel strain if severe
immediate_actions: Distinguish from zoonotic influenza if poultry exposure
payload.who_basis: WHO Global Influenza Programme. ILI definition.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with acute onset of fever ≥38°C AND at least 1 of: cough, sore throat, runny nose — AND at least 1 of: myalgia, headache, significant fatigue.
who_case_definition.probable: Suspected case with positive rapid influenza antigen test.
who_case_definition.confirmed: Influenza virus identified by RT-PCR, culture, or immunofluorescence from nasopharyngeal/throat specimen.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: Not IHR notifiable for seasonal strains. Antiviral therapy most effective within 48h.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Global Influenza Surveillance 2023.

key_distinguishers: Severe symmetrical polyarthralgia — more debilitating than dengue joint pain
Rapid defervescence but arthralgia persists weeks-months
Both Aedes mosquitoes — often co-circulates with dengue
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Chikungunya fact sheet.

sources.title: WHO AFRO IDSR Technical Guidelines 2021 (3rd Ed.)
Mandell, Douglas, and Bennett's Principles of Infectious Diseases, 9th Edition
sources.notes: LR-calibrated symptom weights

sources.title: GeoSentinel / TropNetEurop Travel Medicine Database

payload.name: Chikungunya
key_distinguishers: Severe symmetrical polyarthralgia — more debilitating than dengue joint pain
Rapid defervescence but arthralgia persists weeks-months
Both Aedes mosquitoes — often co-circulates with dengue
payload.who_basis: WHO Chikungunya fact sheet.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with acute onset of fever AND severe joint pain/arthralgia not explained by other medical conditions, in an area with chikungunya risk.
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case with recent travel from endemic area AND positive chikungunya IgM.
who_case_definition.confirmed: CHIKV RNA by RT-PCR, OR ≥4-fold rise in chikungunya neutralizing antibodies, OR virus isolation.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: WHO priority arboviral disease. Not IHR Annex 2 but significant morbidity.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Chikungunya Fact Sheet 2023; PAHO 2011 Guidelines.

key_distinguishers: PREGNANCY RISK — microcephaly + congenital Zika syndrome
recommended_tests: PREGNANCY TEST — if positive, urgent obstetric referral
immediate_actions: ASSESS PREGNANCY STATUS — congenital Zika syndrome risk
case_definition.who_basis: WHO Zika virus fact sheet.

sources.title: WHO AFRO IDSR Technical Guidelines 2021 (3rd Ed.)
Mandell, Douglas, and Bennett's Principles of Infectious Diseases, 9th Edition
sources.notes: LR-calibrated symptom weights

sources.title: GeoSentinel / TropNetEurop Travel Medicine Database
sources.notes: Prevalence calibration in symptomatic returning travellers

payload.name: Zika virus disease
key_distinguishers: PREGNANCY RISK — microcephaly + congenital Zika syndrome
recommended_tests: PREGNANCY TEST — if positive, urgent obstetric referral
immediate_actions: ASSESS PREGNANCY STATUS — congenital Zika syndrome risk
payload.who_basis: WHO Zika virus fact sheet.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person (especially pregnant women) with rash AND fever AND at least 2 of: conjunctivitis, arthralgia, myalgia, headache — in an area with Zika risk.
who_case_definition.probable: A suspected case with positive Zika IgM (cross-reactivity with dengue possible).
who_case_definition.confirmed: Zika virus RNA by RT-PCR in serum/urine (within 7 days of symptom onset).
who_case_definition.ihr_category: PHEIC declared 2016 (ended 2016). Microcephaly risk in pregnancy = urgent priority.
who_case_definition.source: WHO Zika Case Classification 2023. MANDATORY for pregnant women to notify.

recommended_tests: WNV IgM in CSF and serum
case_definition.who_basis: WHO West Nile virus fact sheet.

sources.title: WHO AFRO IDSR Technical Guidelines 2021 (3rd Ed.)
Mandell, Douglas, and Bennett's Principles of Infectious Diseases, 9th Edition
sources.notes: LR-calibrated symptom weights

sources.title: GeoSentinel / TropNetEurop Travel Medicine Database
sources.notes: Prevalence calibration in symptomatic returning travellers

payload.name: West Nile fever
recommended_tests: WNV IgM in CSF and serum
payload.who_basis: WHO West Nile virus fact sheet.

who_case_definition.suspected: Any person with acute fever AND headache ± maculopapular rash ± neurological signs — AND residence in or travel from a WNV-active area during vector season (summer/autumn).
who_case_definition.probable: WNV IgM positive in serum or CSF.
who_case_definition.confirmed: WNV RNA by RT-PCR, OR virus isolation, OR ≥4-fold IgG rise.
who_case_definition.ihr_category: Not IHR notifiable. <1% develop neuroinvasive disease (encephalitis/meningitis).
who_case_definition.source: ECDC WNV Guidance 2023; CDC WNV Case Definition.
